findings.case: GM and Ford industrial counter-intelligence against auto organizing, 1920s Detroit
findings.evidence: GM spent $500,000 a year on 'industrial espionage,' while Ford's factories were patrolled by the notorious Service Department, made up of convicted gangsters and killers
findings.implication: Quantifies the material scale of capital's counter-intelligence investment — a 'Desert'-state surveillance-budget anchor distinct from (and much larger than) anything the underground side could match, forcing indirect channels (anonymous shop papers) rather than direct confrontation.
findings.parameter: $500,000/year (GM, late 1920s-30s) — candidate historical anchor for a capital/state 'surveillance-apparatus budget' coefficient, separate from the mass-relationship-based Intel Confidence term.

findings.case: Get Organized! speech, Border Region production campaign counter-espionage effect, 1943
findings.evidence: In our factories, too, production has been stepped up, secret agents have been combed out and productivity has risen greatly
findings.implication: Mass-organizing (mutual-aid, production campaigns) is credited with directly flushing out infiltrators as a side effect, not through a dedicated counter-intelligence action. Supports EH's design law that only mass-line actions (not SCOUT-like actions) raise intel confidence — organizing itself does double duty as counter-infiltration.

findings.case: Great Terror repression scale, USSR 1930-53
findings.evidence: Executed (1930-53): 786,098 / Imprisoned: 3.5 Million / Death in prison and exile: 2 million
findings.implication: Gives real upper-bound magnitudes for the Repression denominator in P(S|R)=Organization/Repression and for the material stakes of an Intel Confidence failure — when a Desert-state deception collapses, the State's actual repressive capacity that follows is enormous, not symbolic.
findings.parameter: 786,098 executed / 3.5M imprisoned / 2M died in custody over 23 years (~34,000 executions/yr average) — candidate ceiling for repression-intensity coefficient.

findings.case: Guerrilla zone vs. base area taxonomy, anti-Japanese War
findings.evidence: areas which are held by the guerrillas when they are there and by the puppet regime when they are gone, and are therefore not yet guerrilla bases but only what may be called guerrilla zones
findings.implication: A real three-state vision taxonomy predates the design: enemy-held (Desert), contested guerrilla zone (Mud), consolidated base area (Water). Mao's listed transition criteria (enemy destroyed, puppet regime broken, masses aroused, mass orgs formed, political power established) is a concrete formula for crossing the Water threshold.

findings.case: Guerrilla-base martial law counter-intelligence, anti-Japanese base areas, 1938
findings.evidence: The purpose of the former is to suppress traitors and prevent the enemy from getting information
findings.implication: Counter-intelligence (denying the enemy accurate data) was an explicit named military function tied to mass mobilization ('martial law'), not a passive byproduct — supports a deliberate counter-intel action type that degrades an opponent's I_c at a cost.

findings.case: Herman Bernhard, DoJ special agent inserted into the CP local at Buffalo, NY, Jan. 1920
findings.evidence: Mr. Herman Bernhard was appointed as a special agent and not as a confidential informant of the Department of Justice on January 20, 1920, 20 days after the raids on January 2nd.
findings.implication: A mass raid doesn't end infiltration -- the state re-inserted a new agent (who went on to become recording secretary of the Buffalo local) within three weeks. Suggests a short 'agent re-insertion lag' after a state Desert-purge event, not full state blindness.
findings.parameter: Agent re-insertion after a mass raid: 20 days

findings.case: Hippe's legal case against Stahlhelm assault inverted by the court (Naumburg, 1923)
findings.evidence: In this way, the accusers became the defendants.
findings.implication: State institutions (courts, police) don't just withhold information from the Revolution — they can actively re-narrate a documented crime into its opposite. The State's structural narrative advantage over the class enemy is itself a Desert mechanism, independent of any single informant.

findings.case: Hitler recruited as Reichswehr informant, Abteilung I b/P, Munich 1919
findings.evidence: he served as an army stool-pigeon, informing on those among the regular troops who became sympathetic to the workers' movement
findings.implication: State intelligence apparatus builds Intel Confidence not by broad surveillance but by recruiting individual cadre-adjacent informants inside sympathetic ranks — the state's I_c rises via relationship-corruption of the revolution's own base, the mirror image of ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK. Suggests a state-side counter-mechanic: STATE recruits informants from inside high-M_r zones to claw back visibility.

findings.case: Hoover's Jan 2 1920 coded telegram coordinating the Palmer Raids across BoI field offices
findings.evidence: Send to following 33 offices.
findings.implication: The state's surveillance apparatus is bureaucratically omnipresent (33 synchronized offices firing on one script) yet the content is a generic, locally-blind script -- exactly the 'sees everywhere, understands nothing' Desert-state design.
findings.parameter: 33 coordinated field offices synchronized to one nationwide arrest script, timed to a single hour (10am Saturday).

findings.case: Hoover's Oct. 1921 reply confirming existing HUMINT policy
findings.evidence: we made an effort to have an informant in every one of the leading movements of the country, particularly those of a foreign nature
findings.implication: State HUMINT targets leadership/hub nodes, not the rank-and-file mass base — meaning Desert-state masking should be strongest at the base level even for heavily-surveilled organizations, since surveillance itself is hub-biased rather than mass-line.

findings.case: House Committee on Internal Security report on Venceremos/Revolutionary Union, 1972
findings.evidence: We admit there is secret apparatus as necessary. If they have any evidence, let them show it.
findings.implication: A real year-long state-surveillance investigation (HCIS) produced a report naming alleged FBI infiltrators (a married agent couple) and a leadership roster, yet the organization's spokesman only neither-confirms-nor-denies specifics while the report itself misidentifies the org's founder and command structure — the classic 'state sees presence, not relationship' failure: the Desert lens has data but it's wrong.
findings.parameter: ≈1 year HCIS investigation duration before the state produced its (partly inaccurate) surveillance report — reference dwell-time for a Desert-state intelligence product.

findings.case: Irwin Silber (Guardian), analysis of 1960s antiwar/Black-liberation movement fragmentation, 1973
findings.evidence: the conscious infiltration of ruling class agents into the mass movements with the prime purpose of encouraging the nihilistic tendencies of the movement and to prevent any tendency towards a conjunction of the two main mass struggles
findings.implication: Infiltration's strategic target in this account is the SOLIDARITY edge itself (preventing coalition between the antiwar and Black-liberation movements), not just data collection — grounds Desert-state design where falsified/masked data specifically targets relationship formation, not merely observation.

findings.case: Israeli intelligence agents planted among Palestinian detainees, Negev/Ansar III prison, First Intifada era
findings.evidence: Israeli intelligence began paying close attention to this assembly, sending dozens of their agents disguised as detainees
findings.implication: Shows a state achieving deep infiltration even inside a captive, ideologically hostile population by embedding many agents at once, giving an order-of-magnitude density figure for planted-agent saturation of a large, confined mass base.
findings.parameter: Agent-planting density: 'dozens' of embedded agents within one mass-detention population (tens of thousands of detainees).

findings.case: Israeli targeted-assassination campaign against Palestinian faction cadres, West Bank/Gaza, one month (July) during Second Intifada
findings.evidence: attempted ninety-five assassination operations in the West Bank and Gaza Strip, succeeding in about eighty of them
findings.implication: Gives a hard success-rate figure for an intelligence-fed targeted-killing campaign once a state has a full target list - a candidate coefficient for how lethal a Water-level state apparatus becomes against Revolution key figures.
findings.parameter: Informant-fed assassination success rate = 80/95 ≈ 84% in a single month.

findings.case: J. Edgar Hoover intercepts a Comintern cablegram re: CP-CLP unification, Washington DC, Oct. 1, 1920
findings.evidence: I can not make out the meaning of the same. I would appreciate it if you could enlighten me upon the contents of the original cablegram.
findings.implication: The state literally possesses the raw signal (an intercepted directive) yet cannot parse it without outside help — presence without relationship-derived comprehension. A near-perfect real-world illustration of high I_c-from-surveillance but zero true understanding; the Mud/Desert distinction should model 'has the data but not the decode key' as a distinct failure mode from 'has no data.'
findings.parameter: ~7-week relay lag from the Aug. 10, 1920 Comintern decision to the Sept. 25 cable to Hoover's Oct. 1 note — a candidate latency constant for state signal-intelligence pipelines.

findings.case: Japanese imperial intelligence's failed literal infiltration, pivot to disinformation
findings.evidence: they had first sent groups, each consisting of ten "Minsaengdan" members, into the guerrilla army, they had all been captured and executed so that it was impossible to infiltrate into it
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_Reminscences With the Century_ The Anti-Japanese Revolution Volume 4.txt
findings.implication: The enemy's own secret admission: when cadre presence/vetting (a functioning mass line) is strong enough, literal informant insertion fails outright (10-man teams captured every time) — forcing the state to switch tactics entirely from HUMINT to corroding trust between existing cadre (Korean/Chinese, worker/peasant, superior/subordinate). High I_c from a real mass line should mechanically make literal-infiltration actions fail, pushing the state AI toward disinformation actions instead.
findings.parameter: Infiltration cells were sent in units of ~10; all such units were reportedly captured/executed, i.e. 0% success rate for direct-insertion infiltration against a high-cohesion base.

findings.case: Japanese-organized 'Minsaengdan' espionage infiltration of Korean guerrilla base areas in Kando, 1930s
findings.evidence: The Japs organized a counter-revolutionary espionage organization called Mingsaengdan and smuggled it into the revolutionary districts in Kando
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_On eliminating dogmatism and formalism and establishing Juche in ideological work.txt
findings.implication: A real case of an occupying power manufacturing an internal-enemy panic to fracture solidarity from within rather than gathering intelligence directly - a template for a Desert-state event that turns cadre against each other instead of against the state.

findings.case: Jeffrey Paul Desmond, paid FBI agent-provocateur, University of Washington, 1971
findings.evidence: Jeffrey Paul Desmond, a paid FBI agent-provocateur, publicly admitted setting bombs at the University of Washington construction site.
findings.implication: A named, confirmed case of a state agent fabricating a violent 'terrorist' event and framing it on the movement — supports extending the Desert vision-state beyond concealment to state-fabricated FALSE positive events attributed to the Revolution.

findings.case: KMT concentration-camp informant recruitment at checkpoint 'hostels' along the Hsienyang-Yulin Highway and Lunghai Railway, 1939
findings.evidence: arrested progressive young people and intellectuals entering or leaving the Shensi-Kansu-Ningsia Border Region and interned them in concentration camps, where they were either cruelly murdered or compelled to turn informer against the Communist Party.
findings.implication: A documented capture-coerce-turn informant pipeline at named border checkpoints. Infiltration risk should be route-dependent (border crossings between base areas) rather than uniform, and ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK actions crossing hostile-controlled corridors should carry elevated informant-conversion risk.

findings.case: KMT secret-service system denounced in the 'Ten Demands on the Kuomintang' (Yenan mass rally, Feb 1, 1940)
findings.evidence: the secret service is the headquarters of the rumour-mongers and a breeding ground of treason and evil.
findings.implication: Models the State's EH failure mode: a coercive intelligence apparatus that sees everywhere generates volume (denunciations, rumors) but not truthful understanding -- the data is structurally corrupted by fear/reward incentives, supporting a 'Desert' state where surveillance produces falsified-looking-normal readings rather than blank ones.
findings.parameter: 'more than seven hundred progressive young people' interned in KMT concentration camps near Sian -- a concrete scale for state suppression capacity against a cadre-recruitment pool.

findings.case: KPD March Action, March 1921 -- leadership inflates its own reported mass support
findings.evidence: According to the KPD there were one million workers on strike... The number of strikers was actually somewhere between 200,000 and 300,000.
findings.implication: Desert-state falsification can originate from a revolutionary leadership's own propaganda about itself, not only from the State's surveillance apparatus -- I_c inflation by a party's own top-down claims is a distinct failure mode worth modeling.
findings.parameter: Overclaim ratio ~3-5x (1,000,000 claimed vs 200,000-300,000 actual strikers) -- candidate self-report inflation coefficient.

findings.case: Kim Il Sung's corrective doctrine after the Minsaengdan affair and the Pak Hon Yong spy-clique purge, Korea, speech of Dec 1955
findings.evidence: If you do not do so and suspect everybody, in the end you will find yourselves suspicious of your own shadow.
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_On eliminating dogmatism and formalism and establishing Juche in ideological work.txt
findings.implication: Names the exact failure mode an over-classification/Desert mechanic must avoid: treating collapsed Mass Receptivity as universal treason risks the movement destroying its own base faster than any external agent could.

findings.case: Kronstadt Provisional Revolutionary Committee, besieged town, March 1921
findings.evidence: there are still not a few spy-Communists among the populace
findings.implication: Even a rebel mass organ that claims full transparency ('Everything possible is made public') simultaneously withholds military reports and hunts informers in its own ranks -- direct precedent for the Desert vision-state's premise that low-M_r zones aren't just unseen, they're actively counter-surveilled from both directions.

findings.case: Mass reprisal executions and panic-jailings after Nat Turner's rebellion, autumn 1831
findings.evidence: The lowest estimate of the number hung is eleven, given by U. B. Phillips, but most later accounts follow quite closely that given by Thomas Gray.
findings.implication: A single Rupture event triggered a region-wide false-positive cascade (dozens to over 100 people jailed across Virginia, North Carolina, and Louisiana with no connection to the actual plot) — models how one confirmed event should spike apparent, mostly-false threat perception across an entire region, not just the local node.
findings.parameter: 11-19 executed of ~48-53 captured (roughly 1/3 to 2/5) after the rebellion; ~100 more jailed region-wide on unconnected suspicion

findings.case: NASSCO 3 / Ramon Barton agent provocateur, San Diego, 1980-1981
findings.evidence: He was paid, according to sworn testimony, from $5,000-$7,000 to spy on the union.
findings.implication: A concrete agent-provocateur payroll figure tied to a fully documented entrapment (informant supplied bomb parts and an operations manual, wired van, 30 hours of tape). Grounds the Desert vision-state: infiltration doesn't just conceal truth, it manufactures false intelligence (a fabricated conspiracy) that the state then acts on.
findings.parameter: $5,000-$7,000 FBI informant payment; 30 hours of recorded surveillance tape (with a disputed 25-minute gap); resulted in 6-month sentences for the three convicted unionists.

findings.case: Nationwide Espionage Act raids on IWW and Socialist Party offices, Chicago/Detroit/NYC, Sept. 5, 1917
findings.evidence: we wanted to find out whether there have been violations of federal law
findings.implication: The state's raids were a breadth-first fishing expedition, not targeted intelligence: total physical presence (simultaneous raids in many cities) with admittedly no prior comprehension of what would be found. This is the Desert-state pattern exactly — coverage everywhere, understanding nowhere.
findings.parameter: Simultaneous nationwide raids in a single day (Sept. 5, 1917); 8 separate raids in Chicago alone that day.

findings.case: Oskar Hippe -- cumulative years underground/imprisoned across two repressive regimes
findings.evidence: the story of a life that was spent in the prisons of the National Socialists (from 5 January 1934 to the end of 1936) and in those of the East German Stalinists (from September 1948 until 26 July 1956)
findings.implication: Real durations for 'years underground'/imprisoned after arrest under two different apparatuses -- usable calibration anchors for time-to-network-rollup and time-to-reconstitution coefficients.
findings.parameter: Nazi imprisonment ~3 years (Jan 1934-end 1936); East German Stalinist imprisonment ~8 years (Sept 1948-Jul 1956) -- candidate underground/imprisonment duration coefficients.

findings.case: Oskar Hippe, KPD youth organizer, ambushed by Stahlhelm posing as lost travelers (Mücheln, 1923)
findings.evidence: we were addressed by three people in civilian clothing, who asked the way to the station. Since they did not seem suspicious, we told them to come with us.
findings.implication: A textbook Desert-vision-state case: an ordinary, plausible-looking social contact (asking directions) was a masked ambush — the falsified signal 'looked normal' right up to the trap closing (Hippe shot, dragged into a hostile pub). Confirms Desert should model actively deceptive contacts, not merely blank fog.

findings.case: PRRWO/RWL leadership's turn to infiltration-accusation as cover, mid-1976
findings.evidence: the 'left' clique resorted to the cry of being infiltrated by paid bourgeois agents to cover their maneuvering and opportunism.
findings.implication: Infiltration-accusation deployed as an internal information-control weapon when a leadership's real relationship with its own base collapses — a Desert-state tactic turned inward. Textual grounding for the Desert vision-state's 'masked data looks normal' behavior: the accusation itself is the falsification.

findings.case: Palmer Report — prosecution failure despite full surveillance record (El Ariete case, 1919)
findings.evidence: I do not believe that the acts and deeds set forth in the indictment and the evidence given in support of it establish an offense such as this section which I have just read contemplates
findings.implication: Even with a translated manifesto and full case file in hand, the state's own Attorney General judged the intelligence legally inert — data possessed did not convert to comprehension or actionable power. Presence of information is not the same variable as I_c; a Mud-state read can still be strategically useless.

findings.case: Petersen's disinformation triggering the CPA's own secret 'Party Trial' of Louis Fraina, Brooklyn, 1919/1920
findings.evidence: he had also been in close communication with SANTERI NUORTEVA and had told NUORTEVA in order to be in his confidence that FRAINA was an agent of the Department of Justice.
findings.implication: A departing mole's parting act was to seed a false accusation that consumed the movement's own cadre time in a secret internal trial of an innocent leader (named attendees included Lovestone, Bittelman, Hourwich). A Desert/falsified-data event should carry a real opportunity-cost tax on the Revolution side: paranoia and internal witch-hunts, not just lost visibility.

findings.case: Petrogradskaia Pravda's fabricated 'White Guard officer' battle reports vs Kronstadt Izvestia's rebuttal, March 1921
findings.evidence: This is how history is written. This is how the Communists think to hide the truth from the people with slander and deceit.
findings.implication: A concrete Desert-state case: the state press generated a normal-looking counter-narrative (White Guard officers leading cadets) that the besieged garrison's own bulletin had to explicitly rebut point-by-point. Desert-state masking in the game should not just withhold data — it should actively substitute a plausible false narrative, as here.

findings.case: Post Office interception of United Communist Party literature shipments to a Detroit distribution point, April 1921
findings.evidence: On April 7, 1921, a package containing 1000 copies of the Komunista (South Slav), the official organ of the United Communist Party, issue #10.
findings.implication: Mail interception is a pure presence-channel: the state gets precise shipment counts and routes but zero data on who reads the literature or how it lands -- a literal case for the Desert/Water distinction, and for 'you cannot SCOUT your way to intelligence.'
findings.parameter: One week's intercepted volume at a single Detroit address: 500 + 1000 + 25,000 + 5,000 + 50,000 ≈ 81,500 pieces of literature (Apr 7-14, 1921) -- candidate scale for raw-signal-vs-meaning gap.

findings.case: Post-9/11 CVE (Countering Violent Extremism) recruitment of community figures as informants
findings.evidence: CVE programs have sought out teachers, faith leaders, social workers, and family members to become informants monitoring the political beliefs and behaviors of young Black and brown Muslims.
findings.implication: A modern variant where the state doesn't build parallel surveillance but corrupts the existing relationship channel itself — models a distinct EH threat where a cadre-presence node can be 'flipped' to serve state I_c instead of M_r, converting a Water-state relationship into a state asset.

findings.case: Profintern (Red International of Labour Unions) illegal strike-organizing doctrine for fascist/white-terror countries, 1929
findings.evidence: a small group which could continue the leadership of the strike in case of arrests or repressions must be chosen out of the broader strike committee
findings.implication: A real doctrinal design for surviving infiltration: redundant, distributed leadership plus delegate-based (not leader-concentrated) demand presentation, so a single informant or arrest wave cannot decapitate the whole network — directly informs cadre-presence resilience mechanics against Okhrana/COINTELPRO-style rollups.
findings.parameter: Recommended delegation size for presenting demands: 100-200 workers per factory, deliberately excluding all top leading cadre from exposure — a concrete cell-size vs. exposure-risk tradeoff coefficient.

findings.case: Profintern strike-committee recallability clause, 1929
findings.evidence: the right to recall those members of the committee who may afterwards refuse to execute the tasks imposed upon them... thus violating the will of their electors
findings.implication: Recallability functions as a real-time anti-informant / anti-drift mechanism baked into mass organizations, distinct from external counter-surveillance — a design lever for letting mass-line actions include a 'purge the compromised node' option that restores local I_c after a defection.

findings.case: Provisional Government interception of emigrants' telegrams to Russia (spring 1917)
findings.evidence: The telegrams sent to Russia were held up, apparently by our Provisional 'Revolutionary Government' (or its supporters).
findings.implication: Even a nominally 'revolutionary' government actively suppressed inconvenient political information -- Intel Confidence's public-baseline term should not be treated as neutral; official channels can themselves be a falsification source.

findings.case: RCP leadership's suppression of internal dissent over the China-revisionism charge, using coercion, 1977-78
findings.evidence: When the rank and file bolted from this bankrupt scheme and also rejected some aspects of RCP's opportunism on the youth question, thugs were sent in to beat the opposition into submission.
findings.implication: Documents organizational violence deployed specifically as an anti-defection mechanism once a doctrinal shock (the China 'revisionist coup' charge) produced mass internal dissent — models a threshold where a faction facing a receptivity/loyalty collapse switches from persuasion to coercion.
findings.parameter: 'Half of their own rank and file' reportedly rebelled against the CC line within roughly 2 months of the China-charge document's December 1977 publication — a fast defection-fraction/timescale tied to a single external doctrinal shock.

findings.case: RCP's characterization of Soviet-aligned revisionist parties as vectors for infiltrating national liberation movements, mid-1970s
findings.evidence: use their 'socialist' cover to infiltrate, subvert and misdirect the struggle of the working class and movements against colonialism and imperialism to further their own imperialist aims
findings.implication: Infiltration/Intel-Confidence manipulation in this literature wasn't only state-vs-revolution; it was also modeled as inter-faction (a rival power using an ostensibly friendly organization as a Trojan horse) -- the EH mechanic could extend I_c corruption to rival revolutionary factions, not just the State.

findings.case: Revolutionary Political Organization (RPO/ML), a provocateur-led faction purged from the CPUSA/ML, 1979
findings.evidence: the Revolutionary Political Organization (RPO/ML), which was a faction led by provocateurs purged from our Party in 1979
findings.implication: A named, dated real-world case of an agent-provocateur faction operating inside a communist party until detected and purged — usable as a template episode for a scripted infiltration/detection event with a concrete year, corroborating the Malinovsky-type case pattern the design brief asks for.
findings.parameter: Detection lag unspecified but the faction operated long enough to be named and organized before the 1979 purge — candidate reference point for infiltration dwell-time before detection.

findings.case: Roger Baldwin's April 1921 public exposure of the Comintern's 'Charles Edward Scott' cutout identity
findings.evidence: there is no Charles Edward Scott and there is no “Pan-American Agency” of the Third International, which he purports to represent.
findings.implication: Revolutionary organizations also manufacture Mud/Desert conditions for outside observers -- even sympathetic ones like the ACLU -- via pseudonymous cutout identities; deliberate falsification of intelligence is bidirectional, not solely a state tool.

findings.case: Rumor of Augusta Savage's FBI cooperation, Harlem CP-adjacent arts scene, 1940s
findings.evidence: growing rumors about her purported cooperation with the FBI, which had been investigating Black communists in the Harlem arts world, all factored into her decision to relocate
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_A Black Women's History of the United States.txt
findings.implication: Shows unconfirmed suspicion of informancy alone is corrosive to a mass network regardless of truth — M_r can be damaged by rumor even with zero real I_c change, suggesting a distinct 'paranoia' sub-state separate from true Desert.

findings.case: Ryszard Kukliński, Polish General Staff colonel and CIA asset, martial-law crackdown Dec 1981
findings.evidence: they did not even forewarn Solidarity leaders of the impending crackdown although they knew all about the Polish authorities' plans thanks to Kuklinski, a high-ranked Polish officer and CIA agent
findings.implication: Perfect intelligence (a deep-cover asset at the highest level) did not translate into mass survival because the intelligence flowed to an external patron, not through a mass-line relationship to the people it concerned — grounds the design point that I_c is relational, not merely informational: knowing is not the same as the masses knowing.

findings.case: Same campaign's explicit doctrine of bounding the State's own false-positive rate against suspected agents
findings.evidence: To strike surely means to pay attention to tactics. To strike accurately means to avoid wrong executions. To strike relentlessly means resolutely to kill all such reactionary elements as deserve the death penalty.
findings.implication: The State's own counter-infiltration doctrine worries explicitly about wrongful identification. Give the State's anti-infiltration actions a tunable false-positive cost (purging the wrong cadre), and correspondingly give the Revolution's EH mechanic a 'wrongful exposure' risk when M_r misjudges an informant.

findings.case: Seizure of Treasurer Edward Lindgren's papers, United Communist Party of America, NYC, April 1921
findings.evidence: They include the verification of those previous allegations of the government that the United Communist Party is financed from Moscow.
findings.implication: Capturing a single hub-role cadre (the party Treasurer) instantly converted years of state suspicion into confirmed intelligence -- Intel Confidence should have a 'hub node' multiplier: compromising a resource/logistics-role cadre should flip a whole subnetwork from Mud toward Water for the STATE in one event, not gradually.
findings.parameter: Comintern subsidies discovered: $25,000 and $110,000 lump-sum appropriations plus $120,000/year ongoing support to The Toiler -- vs. membership dues of only 60 cents/month, showing external subsidy dwarfing dues income.

findings.case: Smith Act Foley Square trial of 11 CPUSA leaders, New York, Jan-Oct 1949
findings.evidence: a collection of renegade Communists, labor detectives, strikebreakers, professional informers, and outright criminals
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_History of the Communist Party of the United States.txt
findings.implication: Documents falsified intel that 'looks normal' to the adjudicating institution: fabricated testimony (proven perjury by the source's own account) was legally sufficient to convict, matching the Desert vision-state where masked/false data is indistinguishable from real data to the observer.
findings.parameter: Trial-to-conviction processing latency: ~9 months (Jan 17 - Oct 14, 1949) from indictment to guilty verdict on manufactured witness testimony.

findings.case: Sofia Cathedral bombing, 1925 — a French intelligence agent provocateur inside the Bulgarian Communist Party leadership
findings.evidence: the leadership inside the country fell for the clever plot of an agent provocateur, an employee of the French Deuxieme Bureau (Secret Service) and in 1925, exploded a bomb in the Sofia Cathedral
findings.implication: Comintern leadership had explicitly ordered the party to abandon insurrectionary tactics and rebuild the mass base after the 1923 defeat, but a provocateur engineered a false-flag atrocity that reignited state terror anyway — 20,000 more killed. A single high-placed provocateur can override a correct strategic pivot; the game should let an undetected low-M_r/compromised node trigger state repression even after a player has correctly de-escalated.
findings.parameter: Reprisal death toll after the provoked bombing: ~20,000 (on top of ~30,000 already killed suppressing the 1923 uprising).

findings.case: Southern Anhwei Incident / Second Anti-Communist Onslaught, Chiang Kai-shek's posture, March 18 1941
findings.evidence: he is posing as a "national leader" who is above domestic contradictions and feigning impartiality to class and party, his aim being to preserve the rule of the big landlord class and big bourgeoisie
findings.implication: The State (KMT) simultaneously commands total military-surveillance reach (able to launch a surprise attack that nearly annihilated the New Fourth Army) while running a public-facing deception of impartial 'national leadership' — the exact State thesis of EH: omnipresence without honest relationship, masking hostile intent behind a falsified public posture (a Desert-state analog at the elite/diplomatic level).

findings.case: Stanford Daily journalist Phil Revzin's week as a walk-in FBI informer on the Bay Area Revolutionary Union, Nov 1970
findings.evidence: he examined me extensively on personal information, asking for my Social Security number, draft number, driver's license number, parents' names, my height, weight, date of birth
findings.implication: The state's baseline dossier (SSN, DMV, address, family) is comprehensive and instant, yet it produced zero insight into WHY the RU had approached the target — the FBI agent had to ask the informant. This is the Desert/public-baseline split made concrete: presence-data is not understanding.
findings.parameter: Public-baseline I_c should be cheap/instant (identity-registry data) but capped low regardless of coverage, since it structurally cannot answer motive/relationship questions.

findings.case: Stanford student's one-week stint as a walk-in FBI informer on the Bay Area Revolutionary Union, Nov 1970
findings.evidence: Do you still live in trailer 15Z? Is your home address still 7447 Euclid Parkway in Chicago?
findings.implication: The FBI's public-baseline intel (pre-existing personal dossiers: SSN, draft number, address, roommates) was extensive, but the actual informant relationship was shallow and lasted about a week before he 'retired' -- concrete evidence for the thesis that the State sees broadly (high baseline I_c) but understands little without sustained cadre-level relationships.
findings.parameter: informant tenure = ~1 week (single-episode, low-depth infiltration) -- candidate floor value for how briefly an unmotivated informant relationship persists absent real recruitment

findings.case: State repressive reach during the 1922 Shopmen's Strike
findings.evidence: The crowning infamy of this anti-union campaign was the Wilkerson injunction outlawing at one blow 400,000 peaceful strikers.
findings.implication: The state's coercive reach (troops on demand, injunctions 'by the score,' a single injunction covering 400,000 workers) operates through institutional/legal channels rather than cadre relationships — supporting a design choice that state Intel/Control Confidence should sit near-ceiling by default regardless of mass-line relationship-building, unlike the Revolution's intelligence which must be earned.
findings.parameter: 400,000 strikers covered by a single federal injunction (Wilkerson injunction, 1922).

findings.case: Stokely Carmichael on informant paranoia in Black Power organizing, late 1960s
findings.evidence: the question of agents in our community is beginning to make us paranoid. We cannot become paranoid because what they can do is make us so afraid we won't move.
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_Stokely Speaks_ Black Power Back to Pan-Africanism.txt
findings.implication: Fear of infiltration alone, independent of any actual informant, degrades organizational action — suggests a distrust/paranoia variable coupled to but distinct from M_r that can freeze mass-line actions even in Water-state cells.

findings.case: The December 4, 1969 raid pipeline: floor plan to fatal raid
findings.evidence: Mitchell also testified that he obtained a floor plan from O'Neal of the Panther office in June 1969, which led to the June 4 raid.
findings.implication: There were two raids six months apart on the same intelligence relationship (June 4 non-fatal, Dec 4 fatal) — cadre-network compromise isn't a single event but a standing exploitable asset the state draws on repeatedly until the target organization is destroyed. Vision-state 'Desert' should persist and compound, not reset each tick.
findings.parameter: ~6 months between first exploitation of the compromised floor-plan intelligence (June 1969 raid) and the fatal raid it enabled (Dec 4, 1969).

findings.case: The Palmer Raids' nationwide arrest of Socialist/Communist party members, Jan 1920, three months after CP/CLP founding
findings.evidence: The Palmer Red Raids arrested 5,000 to 6,000 Socialist Party members... The raids took place early in January 1920, just three months after the organization of the Party.
findings.implication: A hard number for how fast a newborn underground movement can be rolled up nationwide once the state concentrates attention: a 3-month founding-to-mass-arrest window is a strong candidate for an intel-decay/vulnerability-window coefficient.
findings.parameter: Rollup occurred 3 months post-founding (Sept 1919 -> Jan 1920); 5,000-6,000 arrested nationwide.

findings.case: Third Congress appeal's warning on bourgeois use of agents provocateurs and premature-rising traps, 1921
findings.evidence: it is ready to meet with force any proletarian insurrection and also, if the opportunity presents itself, to provoke a premature proletarian rising in order to smash the class before it has prepared for struggle
findings.implication: Explicit period doctrine on provocation tactics: the state's edge is exploiting organizational impatience to trigger premature action, not superior surveillance data — direct grounding for 'you cannot SCOUT your way to intelligence,' since the counter to provocation is patient relationship-building, not better reconnaissance.

findings.case: White Terror in Finland, 1918 (First Congress Manifesto, March 1919)
findings.evidence: they have helped the bourgeoisie to exterminate twelve to fourteen thousand proletarians and torture to death more than fifteen thousand prisoners
findings.implication: The state's 'Presence' vision (it can see and kill at massive scale, 12-14k executed + 15k+ tortured) requires zero relational understanding of the working class — brute repression is a Desert-state action pattern: total falsified/absent legitimacy narrative, total real physical reach. Confirms EH's core distinction: State sees everywhere, understands nowhere; the two are decoupled even at genocidal scale.
findings.parameter: Finland 1918 White Terror: 12,000-14,000 executed, 15,000+ tortured to death

findings.case: William D. Haywood's bail-jump to Moscow, April 1921 (Bureau agent Loebl's report)
findings.evidence: the officials of the IWW Headquarters had first heard of Haywood's alleged sailing to Russia through the afternoon papers on April 21
findings.implication: Federal agents investigating a heavily-surveilled leadership case had to interview a wire-service editor to learn what happened, because their own network was blind to it — a documented blind spot even against a top target, exactly matching the design intent that presence != relationship/understanding.
findings.parameter: 47 defendants out on bond, ordered to surrender by April 25, 1921; 21 wired confirmation of surrender the same day; 1 of 47 (Haywood) actually skipped — roughly 2% desertion-under-pressure fraction among named defendants.

findings.case: William J. Burns's multi-year embedded operative infiltration of the Iron Workers, 1905-1911
findings.evidence: Burns had operatives planted for several years among the iron workers.
findings.implication: Sustained multi-year embedded infiltration -- not episodic mass raids -- is what actually breaks a movement's core secrets; the engine should distinguish a slow deep-plant intel channel (high yield, long time-constant) from a shallow sweep (Palmer-Raid style, fast but shallow).
findings.parameter: Multi-year (~1905-1911, 5-6 yr) embedded-operative timescale culminating in the 1911 McNamara conviction; resulting labor-movement paralysis persisted roughly a decade ('a blow from which the labor movement has hardly yet recovered' as of 1921).

findings.case: William O'Neal recruited as FBI informant inside the Chicago BPP, 1968-69
findings.evidence: Mitchell recruited O'Neal in 1968 while investigating an auto theft. In exchange for his testimony against the other car thieves, charges were dropped, and O'Neal became a paid informant.
findings.implication: A single embedded cadre-level informant (O'Neal rose to chief of security) can flip a cell's whole vision-state from the movement's Water to the state's Water; infiltration recruitment leverages pre-existing legal exposure rather than random planting.
findings.parameter: Informant recruited ~18 months before enabling the fatal Dec 4 1969 raid; paid "three hundred dollars per month" plus bonuses — candidate per-tick infiltration-upkeep cost coefficient.

findings.case: William O'Neal's infiltration of the Illinois Black Panther Party (Chicago, 1968-1973)
findings.evidence: Mitchell paid him one hundred dollars per week at the outset.
findings.implication: A single paid informant recruited via a minor pretext (auto-theft charge) can operate for years at low cost and become the sole channel of 'intelligence' on a chapter — the game's cadre-presence term should model that a single compromised node can silently zero out real M_r while I_c stays falsely high.
findings.parameter: Informant payment ~$100/week (1968 dollars) at recruitment; O'Neal operated 1968-1973 (~5 years) before public exposure via an unrelated criminal case, not counter-intelligence work by the Panthers themselves.

findings.case: Zinoviev's appeal to Petrograd non-party workers, quoted and rebutted in Kronstadt Izvestia #12
findings.evidence: This provocateur writes, "Let's, in an organized way, arrive at a systematic method of drawing non-party comrades to work."
findings.implication: Both sides of a ruptured relationship accuse each other of running provocation/co-optation operations — the rebels read the party's own conciliatory outreach as a covert intelligence-gathering or pacification ploy. Mechanically: once M_r has crashed, even good-faith mass-line actions (AGITATE_LOCALLY-style outreach) can read as infiltration attempts and fail to raise I_c.

findings.case: Zubatovism -- Tsarist police-sponsored 'legal' labor unions (Okhrana chief Zubatov, 1901-1903)
findings.evidence: Promises of more or less extensive reforms, actual readiness to carry out the tiniest fraction of what has been promised, and the demand to refrain from political struggle in return for this -- such is the essence of Zubatovism.
findings.implication: A real state technique for producing the Desert vision-state at institutional scale: state-run 'worker organizations' generate labor-relations data that reads as normal/positive while actual organizing capacity and relationship-trust are hollow or adversarial -- direct precedent for 'masked data looks normal.'

findings.case: Zubatovism — tsarist police-sponsored 'legal' labor unions (Okhrana operation, early 1900s)
findings.evidence: to the Zubatovs, who are dragging it along the line of clerical and gendarme "ideology"
findings.implication: A real historical case of the State manufacturing a fake mass organization to simulate 'relationship' with workers under police tutelage — the inverse Desert trap: the State builds counterfeit Water-level intimacy via a co-opted front organization rather than genuine cadre presence.

findings.case: 'A Great Revolution That Touches the People to Their Very Souls' (Renmin Ribao editorial, June 2, 1966) framing the GPCR's mass campaign as total ideological penetration with no middle position
findings.evidence: Either you crush me, or I crush you. Either the East wind prevails over the West wind, or the West wind prevails over the East wind. There is no middle road.
findings.implication: Documents the mechanism by which a mass-receptivity-raising campaign escalates into a purge: raising M_r through mass CAMPAIGN mobilization (rather than quiet cadre-building) collapses the vision-state gradient (Desert/Mud/Water) into a binary win-or-be-crushed framing -- campaign-style receptivity gains should carry a destructive-overshoot risk that quiet ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK work does not.

findings.case: Amiri Baraka's COINTELPRO-era prosecution and jailing, Dec 1979-Jan 1980
findings.evidence: Only mass protests in New York and throughout the U.S. helped force Baraka's release three days later
findings.implication: A high-visibility mass base can rapidly reverse a repression outcome already in motion -- suggests a mechanic where organizational Mass Receptivity discounts the effective duration/cost of a repression event rather than only preventing its initiation.
findings.parameter: sentence commuted from 90 days to 3 days served (~97% reduction) via organized mass mobilization

findings.case: Anyuan Colliery strike organizing, Hunan, 1921-1923 (footnote to Four Clean-ups talk)
findings.evidence: After 1921, the Party at first ran spare-time schools for the workers at the mine to carry on Marxist education; then it organized a trade union, which was formally founded on May 1, 1922
findings.implication: A concrete receptivity-build timeline: spare-time schools -> formal union (parallels ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK) -> mass strike four months later, resolved in the workers' favor within five days. Also shows differential resilience: the Anyuan union alone survived the post-Feb-1923 repression wave that destroyed other unions, i.e. invested M_r buys durability under a State crackdown.
findings.parameter: ~4 months from formal union founding (May 1 1922) to mass strike (Sept 10 1922); strike won in 5 days; surviving union was the sole one to withstand the Feb 1923 nationwide crackdown

findings.case: Buffalo CP headquarters raid, Dec 28, 1919, enabled by an unnamed local informant organization
findings.evidence: a local organization, the name of which is not made public, has materially assisted the Lusk Committee in its work here.
findings.implication: What actually punctures a protected relationship network is a planted or sympathetic informant group, not brute police force alone -- this is the historical mechanism for a Water-to-Desert flip via infiltration rather than presence.
findings.parameter: 22 arrested the night of the raid, rising to 52 within roughly 24-48 hours; $2,500 bail per person -- a rollup-speed baseline for post-infiltration network destruction.

findings.case: CCP Central Committee circular on Chungking peace negotiations with Chiang Kai-shek, Aug 26 1945
findings.evidence: You must definitely not rely on the negotiations, must definitely not hope that the Kuomintang will be kind-hearted, because it will never be kind-hearted
findings.implication: A near-verbatim statement of the EH thesis applied to elite-level intelligence: signals from adversary negotiations are untrustworthy by design (Desert-zone falsification), and the only sound Intel Confidence base is 'firmly rely on the people' — i.e. cadre-presence-derived M_r, not diplomatic/positional intelligence.

findings.case: CLP constitution's protection of the National Office's membership/subscriber mailing list, Sept 1919
findings.evidence: The National Office mailing list of locals and branches and of subscribers shall not be given out to anyone outside the membership, nor shall they be given to members for private purpose.
findings.implication: The contact-network list is constitutionally treated as the party's most protected asset -- precisely the 'cadre-presence' resource that drives Mass Receptivity, textually walled off even from members' private use, confirming that relationship-data (not literature or funds) was understood as the core strategic resource.

findings.case: CP/CLP unification schism and dual Central Committees, 1919-1922, per Charles Dirba interview, 1940
findings.evidence: Two Central Committees functioned, each claiming to be the Party — issuing two versions of The Communist with the same masthead.
findings.implication: A real ~3-year latency for organizational/authority confusion (two rival Central Committees, duplicate newspapers) to be fully resolved by successive, initially non-binding Comintern rulings — contrast with the much faster ~5-month 1928-29 Bolshevization correction, suggesting the external-authority mechanism should get faster/more forceful as institutional maturity increases.
findings.parameter: ~36 months (Sept. 1919 split to fall 1922 full resolution) vs. ~5 months (Dec. 1928 Open Letter to May 1929 CC Decision) for the later crisis — a 'Congress effectiveness' maturation curve.

findings.case: CPUSA Organizer's Manual (1935) on why the Shop Unit resists exposure despite factory spy systems
findings.evidence: the Shop Unit will remain the most solid link with the masses under any conditions (terror, illegality).
findings.implication: This is close to a verbatim historical statement of the Water vision-state thesis: intelligence rooted in genuine relationship (a real workplace cell) survives repression that destroys presence-based (public, exposed) intelligence -- 'you cannot SCOUT your way to intelligence' has a direct primary-source antecedent.

findings.case: CPUSA press-office denial in the Poyntz case, Dec. 1937
findings.evidence: we have no record of the woman as a member of the Communist Party and no knowledge of her whereabouts for ten years
findings.implication: An organization can actively falsify (not just withhold) intelligence about its own vanished cadre toward outside inquirers and its own membership — Desert-style falsification can be self-inflicted by leadership onto the rank-and-file, not only imposed by the State.
findings.parameter: Cover-up held privately ~6 months (June 1937 disappearance discovered by her attorney, kept from police) then publicly denied a further ~7 months (Dec. 1937 to mid-1938) — roughly a 13-month full falsification arc before the case broke fully.

findings.case: CPUSA rebuilds underground contact infrastructure after the Palmer Raids, New York, Jan. 1920-Dec. 1921 (per Charles Dirba on C.E. Ruthenberg)
findings.evidence: Ruthenberg organized places to meet District Organizers, codes of communications, addresses for receiving mail.
findings.implication: Rebuilding mass intelligence after a raid required a dedicated 'technician' role (John Schwartz) whose sole job was contacts/places/people-for-conferences — i.e., relationship-infrastructure, not counter-surveillance. This is the concrete operational content of ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK: a named logistics function, not a stat bump.
findings.parameter: ~23 months of full underground operation, Jan. 1920 (Palmer Raids) to Dec. 25, 1921 (Workers Party of America founded as first re-legalization attempt).

findings.case: CPUSA shop-paper organizing in Detroit auto plants, 1920s-30s
findings.evidence: The shop papers' circulation climbed as high as 20,000 in one large plant.
findings.implication: A concrete, single-worksite reach figure for embedded worker-written propaganda under a climate of fear — usable as a calibration anchor for how large a cadre-presence x M_r product can get at one node before intel confidence effects saturate.
findings.parameter: 20,000 circulation in a single large plant — candidate anchor for a local mass-communication reach ceiling.

findings.case: CPUSA's blind zone among enemy-led ethnic mass organizations, per the same 1930 report
findings.evidence: Organizations consisting of over a million members, mostly workers, have no Party members and no influence. These organizations are led by our class enemies.
findings.implication: A literal, numbered Desert zone: over a million workers with zero cadre presence and zero influence, at the same historical moment the Party had real (if thin) coverage elsewhere. Desert should be common at scale, not an edge case -- most of the map should default dark.
findings.parameter: Zero-cadre 'Desert' population documented: >1,000,000 workers, 0 Party members, 0 influence

findings.case: CPUSA's illegal apparatus reconstitution after the Jan. 1920 raids and the Party's brief illegalization
findings.evidence: When I came out they had already established an illegal apparatus. It was March 1920. It was very hard to see people at that time.
findings.implication: Underground relationship-based infrastructure was rebuilt in roughly two months, but contact itself became much harder ('very hard to see people') -- a real historical decay/recovery curve for mass-line intelligence after a severe repression shock.
findings.parameter: Underground apparatus reconstitution time after mass raid: ~2 months (Jan 2 raid -> March 1920)

findings.case: CPUSA/ML doctrine on the Illegal Party and Its Legal Work (Emily Keppler, 'Class Against Class' No.12, Jan 1981), citing Lenin 1901-03 Iskra-period practice
findings.evidence: the organization of the forces cannot be performed in secret even when all work is driven underground
findings.implication: Confirms the EH thesis at the root: hiding structure from the state (illegal) is not the same operation as building real relationships with the masses (which cannot be done secretly). Desert/Mud/Water should key off relationship-building actions, never off concealment actions.

findings.case: CPUSA/ML doctrine, quoting Lenin's 'Illegal Party and Its Legal Work' (Pravda, Nov 7 1912)
findings.evidence: To build the party means strengthening and increasing the number of illegal nuclei, surrounding them by a network of legal strong-points
findings.implication: Gives a concrete structural model for the vision-state architecture: a protected illegal 'Water' core (cadre) ringed by legal 'Mud' contact-points that mediate with the wider public/state-visible layer — a two-tier rather than single M_r gradient.

findings.case: Comintern Third Congress theses on Party organisation (1921) — systematic house-to-house agitation
findings.evidence: Propaganda at the individual level must above all take the form of systematic house-to-house agitation by groups specially established for the purpose.
findings.implication: Mass receptivity in the game should be raised only by dedicated, labor-intensive canvassing actions (AGITATE_LOCALLY), not by any passive or detection-style action — the theses treat door-to-door contact as an organized 'sphere of work' with its own cells, exactly the 'you cannot scout your way to intelligence' design law.

findings.case: Comintern Third Congress theses — weekly cell reporting as the intelligence refresh cycle
findings.evidence: All members must report approximately once a week to the cell or working group to which they belong
findings.implication: Historical underground/mass parties ran their internal intelligence loop on a roughly weekly cadence. This is a plausible historical anchor for how often Intel Confidence should refresh/decay per tick if a tick maps loosely to real weeks.
findings.parameter: Weekly cadre reporting cycle — candidate base tick-length for Intel Confidence refresh in cadre-covered territories.

findings.case: Comintern Third Congress theses — winning trust of semi-proletarian layers as a slow-build process
findings.evidence: This may be a long-term project. The semi-proletarian layers will have more confidence in the Communist movement if the Party takes a sympathetic interest in their day-to-day needs
findings.implication: Receptivity growth rate should not be uniform across class factors — the theses explicitly flag peasant/semi-proletarian trust-building as slower than core-proletarian mobilization, supporting a class-factor multiplier that varies M_r's rate of rise, not just its ceiling.

findings.case: Cost of ideological explanation to the masses, 'Tasks of the Proletariat,' 1917
findings.evidence: To explain this skilfully in a way the people would understand is not easy; none of us would be able to do it at once without committing errors.
findings.implication: Raising Mass Receptivity via AGITATE_LOCALLY/SOCIAL_INVESTIGATION should be effortful and error-prone even for a resourced vanguard — model M_r growth as slow/probabilistic against a much faster decay, not a flat additive gain.

findings.case: Dimitrov/Kolarov exile after the failed 1923 Bulgarian September uprising
findings.evidence: Both were sentenced to death in absentia, both remained outside Bulgaria for 22 years, when they returned within two of months of each other, at the end of 1945.
findings.implication: A failed premature insurrection can put core leadership into multi-decade exile, during which the underground base has to be 'painfully and slowly re-established' from near-zero — a concrete timescale for how long a Desert/collapsed-network state can persist after a catastrophic Adventurism-trap failure.
findings.parameter: 22 years of exile following a failed uprising (1923-1945); underground rebuilding described as requiring sustained, patient reconstruction rather than a quick bounce-back.

findings.case: Ebert–Gröner secret nightly telephone pact, Berlin, 9 Nov 1918
findings.evidence: the High Command will maintain discipline in the Army and bring it peacefully home... to cooperate with the Officer Corps in the suppression of Bolshevism
findings.implication: SPD leader Ebert had a private, trusted, ongoing relationship-channel straight into the military high command (a cadre-network analog) while rank-and-file workers who trusted the SPD press had none into their own leadership's real intentions — a textbook Water-for-elites / Desert-for-masses split running through the SAME organization.
findings.parameter: Channel described as 'regular nightly conversations' — i.e. a daily-tick intel refresh cadence for a high-cadre-presence relationship, contrasted with zero visibility for the mass base.

findings.case: FBI COINTELPRO directive against the Illinois Black Panther Party, Chicago 1969
findings.evidence: "destroy what the BPP stands for," "escalate actions against the BPP," and "destroy the Breakfast for Children Program."
findings.implication: The state targeted the mass-relationship-building program itself, not just weapons or personnel, validating the mechanic's thesis that intelligence/repression should key off mass-line actions (serve-the-people programs) rather than raw force presence.

findings.case: FBI infiltration of the Sojourners for Truth and Justice, 1951-1952
findings.evidence: Government informants riddled the group, enabling the FBI to accumulate more than 450 pages of surveillance files in little more than one year.
findings.implication: A concrete state intel-accumulation rate against a small (132-founding-member) new mass organization: 450 pages within roughly a year — usable as a candidate coefficient for how fast the state's I_c grows against an under-defended org node.
findings.parameter: 450 pages of FBI surveillance accumulated in ~1 year against a 132-founding-member organization

findings.case: Fabricated troop-movement rumor breaks up the Kronstadt Conference of Delegates, March 1, 1921
findings.evidence: This report, completely unexpected by the Conference, was later shown to be false, and was put out by the Communists in the hope of breaking up the Conference.
findings.implication: A directly documented case of the State deliberately injecting false threat-intelligence into an insurgent mass body's decision process, with real behavioral consequences (the ~300-delegate Conference dispersed in alarm) — the literal mechanism of the Desert vision state where falsified data 'looks normal' and drives real action.
findings.parameter: ~300 delegates dispersed by 1 fabricated rumor at a single meeting — candidate magnitude for a Desert-state deception event's behavioral effect size.

findings.case: Fifth Comintern Congress theses on trade-union work (1924) — absence of factory cells as root cause of all intelligence failure
findings.evidence: The basic defect, and the origin of all weaknesses in our trdae union work, is the abscence of party cells in the factories
findings.implication: Corroborates the Mass Receptivity formula's dependence on cadre_presence as a hard multiplier: the Comintern's own post-mortem traces every other listed union-work failure back to this single missing factor, i.e. Intel Confidence should floor near zero wherever cadre presence is zero, regardless of other terms.

findings.case: First feminist samizdat pamphlet, USSR, 1979
findings.evidence: The first feminist samizdat document, Women and Russia: An Almanack to Women about Women, was circulated in 10 copies in 1979
findings.implication: Under total state surveillance, underground informational networks can be viable at almost vanishingly small circulation — a floor parameter for the minimum 'network size' at which a Mud/Water-raising action can still begin propagating, before any exponential growth phase.
findings.parameter: Minimum viable underground circulation observed: 10 copies (1979 USSR feminist samizdat).

findings.case: Four Clean-ups Movement, Hsieh Fu-chih's method vs. work-team human-sea tactic, 1965
findings.evidence: Recruit 20 percent of the people for training. In a factory of 6,000 people, there must be 5,000 that are reliable
findings.implication: Mao's counter-model to bloated external work teams: intelligence/mobilization capacity scales off the ALREADY-reliable mass fraction, not imported outside personnel. Supports an EH design where cadre-presence multiplies existing receptivity rather than substituting for it.
findings.parameter: reliable-mass fraction cited at ~83% (5,000/6,000 workers) as sufficient base to outperform a 500-person external work team

findings.case: Get Organized! speech, Border Region cadre discipline, Nov 29 1943
findings.evidence: 10 per cent of their energy is quite enough for this purpose provided they first devote 90 per cent to helping the masses solve the problem
findings.implication: An explicit numeric ratio for cadre-time allocation between mass-welfare investment and extraction (grain collection): investing 90% of effort in service to the masses is what earns the 10% extraction capacity. A strong literal coefficient for a mass-line action's 'M_r yield per unit cadre-time' formula.
findings.parameter: 90% mass-service effort funds 10% extraction capacity — candidate TL/M_r investment ratio

findings.case: Guardian bureaus converting readers into embedded cadre, 1973
findings.evidence: The bureaus helped bring about a qualitative change in the Guardian's coverage of national events and strengthened its role as a national voice of our movement.
findings.implication: A precedent for how cadre presence converts a passive public-information channel (I_c public-baseline) into a genuinely reliable relational network (higher I_c) — the reader-to-correspondent conversion is the AGITATE_LOCALLY/ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK upgrade path in miniature.

findings.case: IWW job-branch-to-industrial-union information loop via weekly bulletins, described in Industrial Pioneer, May 1921
findings.evidence: This bulletin prints the minutes of all job branch meetings so each branch knows what all others are doing at all times.
findings.implication: A concrete historical mass-line intelligence architecture: weekly, minutes-based (not directive-based) bottom-up reporting, validated by referendum. Gives a real cadence (weekly local, annual/semi-annual industrial convention) to tune SOCIAL_INVESTIGATION/AGITATE_LOCALLY refresh rates against.
findings.parameter: Weekly bulletin cadence at the industrial-union/district level; annual or semi-annual conventions as the higher aggregation/ratification layer.

findings.case: KMT disinformation-terminology invention during the 1945 civil-war run-up, on the eve of Chiang's 'enemies of the people' declaration
findings.evidence: Chiang Kai-shek had already invented many terms, such as 'alien party', 'traitor party', 'traitor army', 'rebel army', 'traitor areas', 'bandit areas', 'disobedience to military and government orders', 'feudal separatism'.
findings.implication: A real Desert-state case where the adversary does not merely hide data but relabels the category system itself so falsified data reads as normal under new, legitimizing terms -- directly matches the EH thesis that Desert-state data is actively falsified, not just absent.

findings.case: KOR strike information exchange run from Jacek Kuroń's father's flat, Warsaw, summer 1980
findings.evidence: This work was carried out by Kuron and a female assistant working from his father's flat in Warsaw.
findings.implication: A 2-person cadre, using only phone/personal-contact relationships, functioned as the national nervous system connecting strikes across an entire country before it scaled into the Inter-factory Strike Committee — supports a very low minimum cadre-count threshold for ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK to start raising M_r region-wide.
findings.parameter: 2-person cadre (Kuroń + 1 assistant) sufficed to run a national mass-line information hub by Aug 1980, before institutionalizing as the MKS (delegates from 20 local strike committees).

findings.case: KPD's Third Period misreading of the September 1930 Reichstag election results
findings.evidence: the previously scorned Nazis, deemed to be 'disintegrating' and therefore hardly worth worrying about, had increased their support by a larger amount than the total Communist vote
findings.implication: A Desert-state case generated from within, not by enemy censorship: fully public election data was available, but doctrinal commitment ('social fascism' as main enemy) produced a falsified reading that looked normal. The mechanic's Desert state should model doctrinal/ideological masking as well as enemy-imposed masking.

findings.case: Kautsky's 'from without' thesis, quoted approvingly by Lenin (1901)
findings.evidence: socialist consciousness is something introduced into the proletarian class struggle from without and not something that arose within it spontaneously
findings.implication: Direct theoretical source for the Epistemic Horizon thesis that intelligence/consciousness must be actively carried in by organized relationship-building (cadre presence), never simply observed or awaited — 'you cannot SCOUT your way to intelligence' is a game-mechanical restatement of this exact claim.

findings.case: Kronstadt Izvestia #12 on differentiated trust in Lenin vs. Trotsky/Zinoviev, March 1921
findings.evidence: Somehow, in the opinion of the workers and peasants, the concept of Lenin on the one hand and Trotsky and Zinoviev on the other came to be different.
findings.implication: Mass trust was personalized and differentiated by named cadre, not uniform across 'the party' as an institution — supports modeling I_c as a function of specific cadre-presence relationships rather than a single organization-wide scalar; a base can retain some residual receptivity to one figure even as it ruptures with the institution as a whole.

findings.case: Kronstadt garrison resolution and Provisional Revolutionary Committee bulletin, March 2-3 1921
findings.evidence: The Communist Party, which rules the country, has become separated from the masses, and shown itself unable to lead her from her state of general ruin.
findings.implication: This is the textbook M_r collapse-to-zero declaration written by the base itself, not the state: intelligence failure is framed explicitly as a broken RELATIONSHIP (separation), not a missing sensor. Confirms EH's core claim that presence (garrison, party cells) without relationship yields false confidence right up to rupture.

findings.case: Land-reform delivery speed vs. peasant loyalty, civil war 1946
findings.evidence: The peasants took a wait-and-see attitude wherever the 'May 4th Directive' was not carried out firmly or the arrangements were made too late
findings.implication: Direct historical instance of M_r's desperation x ideological-alignment term tracking material delivery rather than rhetoric: firm/fast land redistribution produced full mass support, slow delivery produced a documented intermediate wait-and-see (Mud) state.

findings.case: League of Revolutionary Struggle's national agitation-bulletin network during the 1978 postal contract fight
findings.evidence: The bulletins were based on information that workers and League supporters in all parts of the country phoned in. They were compiled at a national center and communicated back to nine cities, where they were printed overnight and distributed
findings.implication: This is a literal ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK infrastructure with measurable throughput: local phoned-in reports, national compilation, sub-24-hour redistribution to a 9-node network -- a concrete real-world model for an intel-network 'refresh cadence' that raises Intel Confidence.
findings.parameter: 13 bulletins / 100,000 total copies issued over ~2 months (July-Sept 1978, roughly 1 every 4-5 days), 9-city distribution network, <24hr compile-to-print latency

findings.case: Lenin on organizing the masses openly rather than secretly, RSDLP 1901-1903 (cited 1980)
findings.evidence: the organization of the forces cannot be performed in secret even when all work is driven underground
findings.implication: Direct historical precedent for the EH design thesis 'you cannot SCOUT your way to intelligence': building real organizational/relational strength among the masses is inherently an open, relationship-based activity even under illegal conditions — only the internal deliberation of cadre can be secret, not the mass-facing organizing itself.
findings.parameter: Cited window: 1901-1903, 'the organization of the forces proceeded illegally, but not secretly.'

findings.case: Lenin's 'vanguard fighter for democracy' argument, What Is To Be Done? III.E, 1902
findings.evidence: To bring political knowledge to the workers the Social Democrats must go among all classes of the population; they must dispatch units of their army in all directions.
findings.implication: Intelligence/consciousness is explicitly relational and requires active outreach beyond one's own class base ('go among all classes'), not passive residence among the workers -- grounds ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK as distinct from simply being present in a territory.

findings.case: Lenin's argument for 'comprehensive political exposures,' What Is To Be Done? III.C, 1902
findings.evidence: These comprehensive political exposures are an essential and fundamental condition for training the masses in revolutionary activity.
findings.implication: Consciousness/receptivity cannot be raised by economic agitation alone or by 'calls to action' from outside -- it requires a specific investigatory practice (exposure of concrete abuses across all classes), which maps directly onto SOCIAL_INVESTIGATION as the only real lever, versus passive scouting.

findings.case: Lenin's justification for adopting a rival party's (S.R.) land mandate verbatim, Oct 26 1917
findings.evidence: As a democratic government, we cannot ignore the decision of the masses of the people, even though we may disagree with it.
findings.implication: Mass-derived intelligence outranks the vanguard's own theoretical preference in this episode -- the game's Water-state data should be honored by policy/AI even when it contradicts the player's ideology, not silently overridden.

findings.case: Lenin, 'Extract from an Article Against the Socialist-Revolutionaries,' December 1902
findings.evidence: The fusion of socialism with the working-class movement (this sole guarantee of a strong and truly revolutionary movement) is no easy matter
findings.implication: Defines 'relationship' (as opposed to mere presence or sympathy) as literal fusion with a class's actual movement — the SR's fault, by contrast, is having no such fusion. Directly grounds the EH thesis line ('you know what the masses tell you') against parties that only claim to speak for classes without organizational fusion.

findings.case: Lenin, 'The Tasks of the Proletariat in Our Revolution,' ch. on Revolutionary Defencism, 1917
findings.evidence: The mass believers in revolutionary defencism are honest, not in the personal, but in the class sense, i.e., they belong to classes
findings.implication: Low mass receptivity to revolutionary appeals is modeled here as structurally honest, not stupid or corrupt: masses can be sincerely captured by an ideology (defencism) that runs against their interest because of imperial/national conditioning. This grounds the 'class-factor' term in M_r = desperation x ideological-alignment x class-factor as a real, not cynical, multiplier.

findings.case: Liebknecht discovers the anti-war opposition's real extent, early 1915
findings.evidence: Liebknecht had to go to Holland and to the various German States in order to be rapidly convinced that the opposition was not restricted to Berlin
findings.implication: Even the revolutionary leadership itself started in a low-I_c 'Mud' state about its OWN base — intelligence about mass sentiment required physical travel and direct contact, not deduction from position or ideology. Confirms the EH thesis that presence (being a famous deputy) does not grant intelligence; only relationship-building trips did — a clean grounding for AGITATE_LOCALLY/ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK as the only I_c levers.

findings.case: Mao's 1953 'Criticize Han Chauvinism' directive admitting the Party's own reporting channels showed false calm
findings.evidence: On the surface all is quiet, but actually there are some very serious problems.
findings.implication: A documented historical admission of a Desert-like reading inside the ruling apparatus itself, correctable only by dispatching real investigation delegations (explicitly NOT 'looking at flowers on horseback') rather than trusting routine reports — strongly supports SOCIAL_INVESTIGATION as the only way to pierce a Desert/falsified reading.

findings.case: Mao's 1957 instruction (Tsinan/Nanking cadre conferences) that army units hold regular subordinate-led criticism sessions to keep officers honest with the ranks
findings.evidence: it would be best to do so once a year by holding criticism meetings for several days in a row.
findings.implication: Gives a concrete cadence for a cheap, recurring mass-line-intelligence-raising ritual (a real-world analog to ESTABLISH_CONTACT_NETWORK/AGITATE_LOCALLY) distinct from the rarer, larger rectification campaign -- supports tiered mass-line actions of different cost/cadence rather than one monolithic 'raise M_r' button.
findings.parameter: candidate baseline cadence for low-cost mass-contact actions: ~once/year, several days duration.

findings.case: Mao's March 1959 Intra-Party Correspondence warning about middle-level cadre filtering during the communal-accounting-unit debate
findings.evidence: Many of these people have become almost completely divorced from the masses and are acting arbitrarily. They feign compliance with, or entirely ignore instructions from higher levels if these instructions do not suit them.
findings.implication: The Desert vision-state can originate INSIDE the state apparatus itself, not just at its edge: mid-level bureaucratic nodes independently filter/falsify reports both upward and downward. Intel Confidence should decay when information passes through untrustworthy intermediary nodes, not only with physical distance from the mass base.
findings.parameter: Mao prescribes cadre conferences of 5-7 organizational levels, convened twice yearly, each running 10 days, specifically to bypass filtered mid-level reporting -- candidate cadence/duration for a 'mass-line audit' action that force-refreshes I_c.

findings.case: Mao's five stated preconditions for the emergence and persistence of Red political power, 1928
findings.evidence: another important condition in addition to the above is required for the prolonged existence and development of Red political power, namely, that the Communist Party organization should be strong and its policy correct.
findings.implication: Cadre-presence/organization strength is explicitly named as one of FIVE conjunctive conditions (alongside inter-warlord war, prior mass mobilization, a live national revolutionary situation, and an adequate regular army) — Intel Confidence / Water-state persistence should be gated on multiple independent conditions, not cadre count alone.

findings.case: March Action combat groups (Max Hölz, Plöttner) -- relationship-based leadership outperforms formal party directive
findings.evidence: These people were the only real participants in the insurrection. The KPD and the KAPD only issued the directives, without having any real influence on the course of events.
findings.implication: Concrete case where real influence tracked personal relationships and local standing, not organizational rank -- supports weighting cadre-presence over formal hierarchy in the Intel Confidence formula.

findings.case: Marighella's Minimanual doctrine of information as popular support (1969)
findings.evidence: Information, which is only a small segment of popular support, represents an extraordinary potential in the hands of the urban guerrilla.
findings.implication: Explicitly equates intelligence quality with popular support rather than surveillance reach — directly confirms Intel Confidence should scale with Mass Receptivity (cadre-presence × M_r) rather than with any 'scouting' action.

findings.case: Mass pressure forcing a leadership peace appeal despite its own preferences (Petrograd Soviet, March 14 1917)
findings.evidence: Socialist-Revolutionary and Menshevik leaders were obliged to issue this appeal under pressure from the revolutionary masses, who demanded an end to the war.
findings.implication: Doctrine/policy at the top can be forced upward from measured mass mood rather than flowing down from leadership choice -- intel about mass receptivity here is not just an input to strategy, it visibly overrides the strategic preference of the nominal leaders who hold it.

findings.case: Minsaengdan psychological-warfare campaign against Kim Il Sung's guerrilla zones (Jiandao, 1932-1935)
findings.evidence: In order to ferret out those seven or eight, the "purge" campaign had massacred more than two thousand friends on the false charge of involvement in the "Minsaengdan".
findings.source: /media/user/data/old-hdd/old-hdd/prolewiki/Exports/Library/Library_Reminscences With the Century_ The Anti-Japanese Revolution Volume 4.txt
findings.implication: This is a real historical 'Desert' state weaponized against the revolution itself: falsified intelligence (rumors of mass infiltration) drove the organization to destroy ~250x more of its own cadre than the actual infiltrator count, over three years, before top leadership intervened to correct it.
